title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Colombia — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Tinto is your daily ritual
items.text: A tinto is a small black coffee served in a demitasse cup—it's what Colombians drink throughout the day, not espresso. Order it at bakeries, offices, and street vendors; it costs 2,000-4,000 pesos and comes with a small piece of bread or pastry.

items.label: Visit a finca or coffee museum in the Eje Cafetero
items.text: The coffee triangle (Armenia, Pereira, Manizales) is where you'll see coffee actually grown and processed. Most fincas offer day visits with tours, tastings, and meals; book through your hotel or directly to avoid middlemen markups.

items.label: Café con leche is breakfast coffee
items.text: This is hot milk with a shot of coffee, served in a large cup or bowl. Colombians drink it with arepas, bread, or pastries at breakfast; it's different from a simple cortado and is considered a complete morning drink.

items.label: Skip the 'Juan Valdez' chain if seeking authenticity
items.text: These cafés are tourist-oriented and overpriced. Instead, find local cafeterías or street vendors serving fresh tinto, or visit a specialty roaster in Bogotá or Medellín for single-origin beans and proper preparation.

items.label: Arepas (corn cakes)
items.text: These are ubiquitous, affordable, and endlessly variable—filled with cheese, meat, eggs, or avocado. Get them from street vendors, small restaurants, or at breakfast; they're cheap (3,000-8,000 pesos) and genuinely delicious.

items.label: Bandeja Paisa (Medellín's signature platter)
items.text: An enormous plate of beans, rice, ground meat, fried egg, sausage, arepa, and more—it's a full meal for one hungry person or two to share. It's filling and should cost 25,000-35,000 pesos; this is authentic regional comfort food, not tourist bait.

items.label: Sancocho (hearty stew)
items.text: A slow-cooked stew with chicken or beef, plantain, yuca, and vegetables—it's soul food in Colombia and appears at family tables and comedores alike. Order it at lunch; it's cheap, filling, and tastes different in each region.

items.label: Ceviche (seafood appetizer)
items.text: Fresh fish cured in lime, served with coconut milk, lime juice, and cilantro—it's popular on the coasts. Try it in Cartagena or Santa Marta; coastal ceviches are fresher and cheaper than versions inland.

items.label: Patacones (fried plantain rounds)
items.text: Green plantains are sliced, fried, flattened, and fried again until crispy. They come with every meal on the coast and are addictively salty; often served with a garlic sauce called ají.

items.label: Empanadas (fried pastry pockets)
items.text: Filled with meat, cheese, or potato—they're street food, cheap (2,000-4,000 pesos), and made fresh throughout the day. Quality varies wildly; buy from busy vendors with queues.

items.label: Look for the 'comida del día' (set lunch)
items.text: Most restaurants offer a fixed lunch menu (12pm–2pm) with soup, main, drink, and dessert for 12,000–18,000 pesos. It's the best value in the country and a window into what locals actually eat.

items.label: Menu terms: entrada, plato fuerte, postres
items.text: Entrada is an appetizer, plato fuerte is the main dish, and postres is dessert. Colombians don't eat large appetizers; a simple salad or soup is typical, then a big main course.

items.label: Ask 'qué me recomienda?' (what do you recommend?)
items.text: Staff will steer you toward the day's best dish, often something freshly prepared. This is a natural question and usually leads to better food than what's on the printed menu.

items.label: Bread and butter may not come automatically
items.text: If bread (pan) is served, you'll be charged. Don't assume it's complimentary; many restaurants ask if you want it before serving.

items.label: Specify 'sin picante' if you can't handle heat
items.text: Colombian food isn't typically spicy, but some regions use hot peppers (ají). If you're sensitive, ask the server when ordering; they'll prepare the dish mild.

items.label: Comedores (worker's cafés)
items.text: These small, no-frills eateries serve the comida del día for 12,000–16,000 pesos; they're packed with locals at lunch and close by 3pm. Look near markets, bus stations, or office buildings—quality is high, atmosphere zero.

items.label: Street food: arepas, empanadas, fresh fruit stands
items.text: Buy from vendors with constant customer turnover, not stationary carts. Fruit stands are excellent for fresh jugo (juice); ask for batido if you want a blended smoothie rather than juice.

items.label: Restaurants in La Candelaria (Bogotá) and El Poblado (Medellín)
items.text: Tourist neighborhoods with reliable food at moderate prices (20,000–40,000 pesos for a main). Quality is good but markup is real; venture outside these zones to neighborhoods like Usaquén or Laureles for better value.

items.label: Avoid English menus in city centers—they're tourist traps
items.text: Restaurants with English-language menus on the street often inflate prices by 50–100%. Ask a local where they eat, or walk 2–3 blocks away from main plazas and tourist streets.

items.label: Seafood on the coasts: Cartagena, Tayrona, Providencia
items.text: Fish, ceviche, and coconut dishes are fresh and cheap near the Caribbean. Eat where you see fishermen; avoid waterfront tourist restaurants unless you're willing to pay heavily for ambiance.

items.label: Markets like Paloquemao (Bogotá) have food stalls
items.text: These are chaotic but authentic; buy pre-cut fruit, juice, and simple meals from vendors. Go during early morning hours for the best selection and energy.

items.label: Agua de panela (unrefined sugar water)
items.text: A warm drink made from blocks of unrefined sugar, sometimes with lemon or ginger. It's served free in many rural areas and comedores, considered therapeutic, and tastes like sweet tea.

items.label: Fresh jugo and batidos (juices and smoothies)
items.text: Fruit stands serve fresh-squeezed orange, papaya, and passion fruit juice for 3,000–5,000 pesos; ask for a batido if you want milk blended in. Juice quality is excellent but hygiene varies—drink from busy vendors.

items.label: Aguardiente (Colombian spirit)
items.text: A strong anise-flavored liquor (29–60% ABV) popular for celebrations and shots. It's harsh on its own; locals drink it mixed or with ginger ale. A bottle is cheap (15,000–25,000 pesos).

items.label: Cerveza (beer)
items.text: Local brands like Aguila, Poker, and Club Colombia are light lagers; a bottle or can costs 2,000–5,000 pesos in stores, more in bars. Colombian craft beer is growing in Medellín and Bogotá (5,000–10,000 pesos per beer).

items.label: Wine is expensive; Colombian rum is better value
items.text: Wine is imported and costs 50,000+ pesos in restaurants. Instead, try local rum (Ron Viejo de Caldas, Diplomático) for cocktails or neat; it's better quality and cheaper than wine.

items.label: Chicha (regional corn drink)
items.text: A sweet, thick drink made from corn and cinnamon—it's traditional in Bogotá but harder to find now. If offered, try it; it's an acquired taste but genuine cultural experience.

items.label: Tap water is safe in major cities but stick to bottled water outside
items.text: In Bogotá, Medellín, and Cartagena, tap water is treated and safe. In smaller towns and rural areas, buy bottled agua sin gas (still) or con gas (sparkling) from stores.

items.label: Lunch is the main meal, dinner is light
items.text: Colombians eat a big lunch (11am–2pm) and a lighter dinner around 7–8pm. Many restaurants have different pricing at lunch vs. dinner; lunch is cheaper and busier.

items.label: Tipping is not obligatory but appreciated
items.text: Service charge is usually not included; round up the bill or leave 5–10% for good service in sit-down restaurants. Street food and comedores don't expect tips.

items.label: Eat arepas and bread with your hands
items.text: Arepas, empanadas, and most street food are meant to be eaten by hand. Using a fork feels awkward; locals will eat it casually.

items.label: Say 'buen provecho' before eating or when leaving
items.text: This is the standard greeting when someone sits to eat or when leaving a table—servers will say it to you, and it's polite to say it to others around you. It means 'good appetite.'

items.label: Accept food and drink offered by hosts graciously
items.text: Refusing offered food can be seen as rude. Even if you're not hungry, take a small portion or sip; declining entirely may offend your host.

items.label: Finish your plate or leave a small amount
items.text: Leaving a clean plate is a compliment to the cook. Leaving a lot uneaten implies the food was bad; if you're full, it's better to leave just a few bites.

items.label: Don't eat on public transport or streets if you look like a tourist
items.text: While locals do it, eating visibly as a foreigner can attract unwanted attention. Eat in restaurants or quiet spots instead, especially at night or in unfamiliar areas.
